sound_type: bugle -- high screaming whistle (1,000-4,000 Hz) breaking into guttural grunts (100-300 Hz). Two-part vocalization. Fall rut. E=7
ecosystem: Old-growth forest, river valleys, subalpine meadows. Olympic Peninsula, Cascade Range. Fall rut September-October.
season: Fall rut September-October. Bulls bugle to announce presence, challenge rivals, attract cows.
family: Cervidae (third entry -- deer fawn 195, deer buck 196)

coordinates.description: Roosevelt Elk on the Olympic Peninsula. Hoh Rainforest, Queets, Quinault valleys. Also Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: First Roosevelt Elk entry. Third Cervidae in the engine. Elk trio begins (197-199): bugle, cow call, rut roar.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Cervidae: Deer Fawn (195, E=3), Deer Buck (196, E=4), Elk Bugle (197, E=7).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cervus from Latin 'deer'. canadensis for Canada. roosevelti for Theodore Roosevelt (Merriam 1897).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cervus -- true deer. Includes elk (wapiti) and red deer.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cervidae: third entry. Elk trio begins.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Class Mammalia. Largest PNW land mammal. 300-500 kg.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 100-4,000 Hz (full bugle span: grunt to whistle peak).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Bugle: 2-5 sec complete. Repeated at 1-10 minute intervals during rut.
song_metrics.note_structure: Two-part: ascending whistle then descending guttural grunts.
song_metrics.note_types: Bugle whistle (high, ascending), bugle grunt (low, pulsed), bark (alarm).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Complete bugle = whistle + grunt sequence. Repeated during territorial patrol.
song_metrics.timbre: Whistle: metallic, piercing. Grunt: rough, guttural, chest-resonant.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=7. One of the loudest mammalian vocalizations in the PNW. Carries 2-5 km in valleys.
song_metrics.tempo: Bugles at 1-10 minute intervals during active display. Faster when rival present.
song_metrics.context: Fall rut (September-October). Bull territorial display. Rival challenge. Cow attraction.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Roosevelt Elk bugle slightly lower than Rocky Mountain Elk due to larger body size.
song_metrics.key_property: BUGLE. Two-part vocalization. Widest frequency range of any single PNW mammal. E=7. ELK TRIO begins 197-199. v1.49.338.

pelage.overall: Adult bull: dark brown body, lighter tan rump patch, dark brown mane on neck. Antlers: up to 6 points per side (mature). Stockier and darker than Rocky Mountain Elk.
pelage.diagnostic_mark: Large body size (300-500 kg), dark coloring, prominent antlers (fall), tan rump patch, dark neck mane.
pelage.seasonal_variation: Summer: darker brown. Winter: slightly lighter. Antlers: spring growth through fall hardening to winter shedding. Neck mane thickens during rut.

physical_description.overall: Adult bull: 300-500 kg (700-1,100 lbs). 130-150 cm at shoulder. Antlers: up to 120 cm span. Largest elk subspecies. Largest PNW land mammal.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Massive body. Antlers (bulls, fall). Dark brown coloring. Tan rump. Descended larynx (enables wide-range bugle).
physical_description.field_id: Very large brown ungulate with antlers (bulls). Much larger than deer. In groups of 10-60 in meadows and river valleys. Listen for bugle in September-October: unmistakable high whistle breaking into grunts.

nodes.title: Two-Part Bugle -- Whistle + Grunt
nodes.description: High whistle (1,000-4,000 Hz) breaking into guttural grunts (100-300 Hz). Widest frequency range of any single PNW mammal vocalization. Descended larynx enables both extremes.

nodes.title: Old-Growth Acoustic Chamber
nodes.description: Cathedral-like old-growth forest amplifies the bugle. Tall canopy, open understory, soft floor. The whistle carries through massive trunks.

nodes.title: Elk Trio -- 197-199
nodes.description: Three consecutive degrees of Roosevelt Elk: bugle (E=7), cow call (E=5), rut roar (E=7). Most sustained single-species presence in the engine.

nodes.title: Roosevelt -- Conservation Naming
nodes.description: Named for Theodore Roosevelt (Merriam 1897). Largest elk subspecies. Primary reason for Olympic National Park creation.

cross_references.relationship: CERVIDAE PROGRESSION! Deer fawn (E=3) and buck (E=4) to elk bugle (E=7). Body mass scales with vocal power.
ELK TRIO! Second elk entry at 198. Cow bark + mew at E=5. Maternal communication.
E=7 MATCH! Blue Whale (E=7, ocean) and elk bugle (E=7, mountain valley). Different media, similar environmental power.
VOICE PAIR! SDRE (E=5) + elk bugle (E=7). Both voices that cannot be contained. E=12 combined.
